ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mucor-fermented hairy tofu (Mao Doufu). Closest match is the general fermented tofu food product class. Exact term unavailable as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers fermented soybean products broadly. No specific code exists for Mucor-fermented hairy tofu. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hairy tofu or Mao Doufu as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of hairy tofu is approximately 120 g, equivalent to two to three pan-fried or deep-fried pieces served with a spicy dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu, firm, prepared with nigari (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #16427) adapted for fermented hairy tofu preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Mucor-fermented hairy tofu (Mao Doufu). Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy firm tofu baseline (FDC #16427) with adjustments for fermentation-driven protein hydrolysis, fat absorption from pan/deep-frying, and elevated sodium from added soy sauce and fermentation salts. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against this USDA baseline and retained where consistent. Vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg/100 g) is elevated relative to plain tofu and reflects microbial synthesis during Mucor fermentation; this is plausible but should be confirmed by direct laboratory analysis. Saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on soybean oil fatty acid profile from USDA. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in the adapted source for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soy and fermented soy products — analogous estimate based on plain tofu GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mao Doufu (hairy tofu) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 20 is estimated by analogy with plain firm tofu (GI approximately 15–20, University of Sydney GI Database) and accounts for the low residual carbohydrate content (4.8 g/100 g) after Mucor fermentation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 5.76) / 100 ≈ 1.2. This is a low glycemic load. Estimation basis is documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg/100 g) from fermentation salts and soy sauce-based dipping sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit hairy tofu consumption due to its high sodium content of 420 mg per 100 g; a single 120 g serving contributes approximately 504 mg sodium, which is over 20% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and sauce should be minimised or omitted.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (4.8 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (estimated GI 20) with high protein (10.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hairy tofu is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes as its low carbohydrate content and estimated low GI of 20 produce minimal postprandial glucose excursion; however, sodium from accompanying sauces should be monitored to avoid compounding cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and fat from deep-frying offset the cardiovascular benefit of soy isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While soy protein and isoflavones in hairy tofu may offer modest LDL-lowering benefits, the high sodium content (420 mg/100 g) and fat absorbed during pan- or deep-frying elevate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume hairy tofu in small portions, preferably pan-fried with minimal oil and without high-sodium dipping sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones and fermentation-derived bioactive compounds with potential anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fermented soy products such as hairy tofu contain isoflavones and may harbour probiotic-like microbial metabolites that have been associated with modest anti-inflammatory effects; consumption in moderate portions is generally acceptable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hairy Tofu (毛豆腐, Mao Doufu) is a traditional fermented tofu specialty originating from the Huizhou region of Anhui Province in eastern China. It is produced by fermenting fresh pressed tofu in a temperature- and humidity-controlled environment until white, thread-like mycelium of Mucor fungi colonise the surface, giving the block a distinctive furry or 'hairy' appearance. The fermentation process hydrolyses soy protein into free amino acids and peptides, producing a deep, savoury umami flavour and a soft, creamy, cheese-like interior texture reminiscent of a mild blue cheese. The most common preparation is pan-frying or deep-frying until the mycelium collapses into a golden-brown, lightly crispy skin while the interior remains silky. It is traditionally served with a spicy and salty condiment containing chili paste, scallions, and soy sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 165 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 11.5 g fat, and 4.8 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 420 mg largely attributable to the fermentation salts and accompanying sauce. Its estimated glycemic index of 20 places it firmly in the low-GI category, making it appropriate for blood sugar management. The high sodium content makes it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Hairy tofu is considered a culinary treasure of Anhui cuisine and is classified as a processed fermented soy product.

How is Mao Doufu different from regular tofu?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Mao Doufu (hairy tofu) exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central firm tofu baseline with adjustments for fermentation and frying. The AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this USDA baseline and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database plain tofu data. Vitamin B12 value (1.5 mcg/100 g) is plausible for Mucor-fermented products but requires laboratory confirmation. Overall confidence is medium pending direct analytical data.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pan/deep-fried Mucor-fermented hairy tofu is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific fermented and fried preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or approved sources as of 2024. 3. Vitamin B12 at 1.5 mcg/100 g reflects microbial synthesis during Mucor fermentation and is plausible but should be confirmed by direct laboratory analysis; plain tofu contains negligible B12. 4. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from soybean oil fatty acid profile per USDA. 5. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as soybean-based frying oils used in traditional preparation contain negligible industrial trans fats. 6. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 as tofu is a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol. 7. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate based on plain firm tofu GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mao Doufu is available. 8. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 120 g entries) was collapsed to a single canonical serving of 120 g. 9. Halal status marked false due to uncertainty about fermentation process compliance; should be verified with a certified halal authority.
